data.content_ref: inline:{"claim": "No fixed (base error, precision, recall, k) regime is sufficient; harness strictly beats SC only when the critic's per-question confidence is positively correlated with the SC winner being wrong, so that filtering selectively removes wrong-majority items more than right-majority items.", "mechanism": "Define critic-winner alignment A = P(critic flags | SC winner wrong) - P(critic flags | SC winner right). This is question-conditional and subsumes precision/recall, which are pool-level aggregates that wash out per-question signal. Harness strictly beats SC iff A > threshold, where threshold depends on the flip-vulnerability of correct winners. Two critics with identical pool-level precision and recall can have opposite A values: one flags wrong winners preferentially (A > 0, harness gains), the other flags randomly or anti-correlatedly (A <= 0, harness ties or loses). The measured data is explained: v4-pro hard-set had A undefined (no wrong winners to condition on), v4-flash easy-set had A near zero (critic flags uncorrelated with winner correctness, precision 0.333 means most flags hit correct winners).", "scope": {"covers": ["question-conditional critic-winner alignment as the operative variable", "any base error rate where wrong SC winners exist (e > 1/(k+1))", "both high and low pool-level precision/recall, if alignment is favorable"]}, "excludes": ["claims that fixed (P, R) values alone predict harness advantage", "regimes where no wrong SC winners exist (e <= 1/(k+1))", "critics whose flagging is independent of winner correctness (A=0)"]}, "forbidden": [{"case": "Two critic configurations with matched pool-level precision ~0.5 and recall ~0.5 but different alignment A (A_high > 0.3 vs A_low < 0.1) produce statistically indistinguishable harness-vs-SC gaps, refuting the alignment thesis", "eval": "rubric:std-crit-design"}, {"case": "A critic with higher alignment A but lower precision AND lower recall than another critic produces a larger harness-vs-SC gap, confirming alignment dominates precision/recall", "eval": "rubric:std-crit-design"}, {"case": "Alignment A > 0.3 does NOT produce strict harness advantage in >= 60% of studies when base error is in [0.15, 0.35], falsifying the A > threshold prediction", "eval": "rubric:std-crit-design"}, {"case": "The correlation between A and harness-vs-SC gap across all study cells is r < 0.3, while the correlation between pool-level precision and the gap is r > 0.5, contradicting the subsumption claim", "eval": "rubric:std-crit-design"}], "prose_notes": "Extends c9386a6c and 30e483eb by proposing a specific measurable quantity (alignment A) and a decisive experiment that controls for precision/recall while varying alignment. The experiment uses 300 questions at a single base-error tier (e ~ 0.25, k=5) where wrong winners are guaranteed to exist, and generates 6 critic variants via different prompting strategies (e.g., 'flag if the answer seems too simple' vs 'flag if you detect a specific error type') that produce different alignment values at similar precision/recall. ~50k tokens per variant, 300k total. The counterexample-first stance is honored: the forbidden cases include direct refutations of the alignment thesis and tests of whether pool-level precision outperforms alignment as predictor."}
